elements.sent-2411-001: Static structured control refers to the task of designing a state-feedback controller such that the control gain satisfies a subspace constraint.
elements.sent-2411-002: Structured control has applications in control of communication-inhibited dynamical systems, such as systems in networked environments.
elements.sent-2411-003: This work performs $H_2$-suboptimal regulation under a common structured state-feedback controller for a class of data-consistent plants.
elements.sent-2411-004: The certification of $H_2$-performance is attained through a combination of standard $H_2$ LMIs, convex sufficient conditions for structured control, and a matrix S-lemma for set-membership.
elements.sent-2411-005: The resulting convex optimization problems are linear matrix inequalities whose size scales independently of the number of data samples collected.
elements.sent-2411-006: Data-driven structured $H_2$-regulation control is demonstrated on example systems.
elements.sent-2411-007: When designing control policies for dynamical system, it is often desired that the controller respect a given structural property.
elements.sent-2411-008: In a state-feedback context, the gain matrix $K$ of the control policy $u = K x$ must respect a subspace constraint $K \in \s$, in which $\s$ encodes the desired structure of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-009: Structured control with subspace constraints is generically an NP hard problem \cite{blondel1997np .}
elements.sent-2411-010: A specific instance of structured control is decentralized control, in which the controller must match a sparsity pattern derived from the information/actuation structure of the overall system \cite{wang1973stabilization, bakule2008decentralized .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Decentralized control can be convex in the case where the subspace $\s$ possesses a property of quadratic invariance (QI) \cite{lessard2011qi , but QI-based convex control schemes are only nonconservative if dynamic feedback laws are permitted (rather than static output feedback).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Network decompositions can be performed to reduce the conservatism of decentralized control methods, which would in turn be nonconservative under dynamic output feedback and QI \cite{furieri2018robust .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The work in \cite{furieri2019separable performs iterative merging to create separable quadratic Lyapunov functions in the distributed (static state feedback) control setting.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The work in \cite{ferrante2019design, ferrante2020lmi offer convex but conservative formulations for state-feedback structured control in the stabilization and $H_\infty$-norm minimization settings.}
elements.sent-2411-015: This work focuses on finding a worst-case $H_2$-regulating structured control for all systems that are compatible with observed data using the framework of set-membership data-driven control.
elements.sent-2411-016: The methodology of data-driven control treats the data itself as a model of the system, and then synthesizes control parameters based on the data without first passing through a system identification routine \cite{HOU20133, hou2017datasurvey .}
elements.sent-2411-017: The idea behind such approaches is the fact that finding a control law is often easier than describing a suitable model for a plant.
elements.sent-2411-018: One approach is the set-membership style of data-driven control, which requires describing:.
elements.sent-2411-019: The set of plants regulated by a given control policy;.
elements.sent-2411-020: The set of plants consistent with the observed data under an a-priori-known noise bound;.
elements.sent-2411-021: A certificate of set containment such that every plant consistent with the data is also regulated.
elements.sent-2411-022: This paper will describe the set of plants consistent with the data by a matrix ellipsoid as defined by a \ac{QMI constraint \cite{van2023quadratic .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The work in \cite{van2020noisy demonstrates how to derive guarantees for stabilization, worst-case $H_2$ suboptimal control, and worst-case $H_{\infty}$ suboptimal control over these \ac{QMI -defined sets through the use of a matrix S-Lemma \cite{yakubovich1997s .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: These \ac{QMI -based results have also been used for control of networked systems under block-diagonal partitioning in \cite{eising2022informativity, wang2023event and for pole placement in LMI-defined regions ($D$-stability) \cite{bisoffi2022learning .}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Other instances of set-membership data-driven control include the formation of polytopic Lyapunov functions (e.g. superstability) \cite{cheng2015, miller2023positive with certifications arising from an Extended Farkas Lemma \cite{hennet1989farkas .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Methodologies for data-driven control that require a model reference include virtual reference feedback tuning \cite{campi2002virtual , \cite{bazanella2011data , iterative feedback tuning \cite{hjalmarsson1998iterative , and correlation-based tuning \cite{karimi2004iterative .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: A particularly popular technique for data-driven control is the behavioral approach based on Willem's Fundamental Lemma \cite{willems2005note , in which the space of all feasible trajectories of a linear system can be expressed as a linear combination of observed data (in Hankel matrix form) if a persistency of excitation rank condition is obeyed.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This Fundamental Lemma has been employed for model predictive control \cite{waarde2020informativity, depersis2020formulas, coulson2019data, berberich2020mpc , stochastic control \cite{pan2022stochastic , and closed-loop control \cite{DINKLA20231388 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The work of \cite{celi2023data performs sparse (structured) control design of a feedback gain $K$ in the behavioral setting under the absence of noise.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The contributions of this paper are:.
elements.sent-2411-031: A centralized LMI formulation for data-driven $H_2$ regulation using structured control based on the sufficient-convex constraint of \cite{ferrante2019design ;.}
elements.sent-2411-032: An accounting of computational complexity for the presented conditions;.
elements.sent-2411-033: Demonstrations of $H_2$-suboptimal structured control on example systems.
elements.sent-2411-034: This paper has the following structure: Section \ref{sec:preliminaries reviews preliminaries such as notation and (nominal) structured control; Section \ref{sec:data_driven describes consistency of structured-stabilizing-plants and data-consistent plants, and develops an \ac{LMI for data-driven certifiable $H_2$-suboptimal structured control; Section \ref{sec:examples performs demonstrations on example systems; and Section \ref{sec:conclusion concludes the paper.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The set of $m \times n$ real-valued matrices is $\R^{m \times n}$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The transpose of a matrix $M \in \R^{m \times n}$ is $M^\top \in \R^{n \times m}$.
elements.sent-2411-037: The set of $n \times n$ square symmetric matrices is denoted $\psd^n$.
elements.sent-2411-038: If $P \in\psd^n$, we write $P\succeq 0$, and $P \succ 0$ if P is positive semidefinite or positive definite, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-039: The sets of positive semidefinite and positive definite matrices are denoted $\psd^n_+$ and $\psd^n_{++}$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Kronecker product between matrices $A$ and $B$ is $A \otimes B$.
elements.sent-2411-041: The symbols $I_n$, $\0_{n \times m}$ and $\1_{n \times m}$ will refer to the identity, zeros, and ones matrix respectively.
elements.sent-2411-042: The dimensions will be omitted if there is no ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-043: Given a transfer matrix $G(z)$, we denote its $H_2$-norm by $\norm{G(z) _{H_2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This work considers discrete-time linear systems with state $x \in \R^n$, input $u \in \R^m$, exogenous input $\xi \in \R^z$, and measured output $y \in \R^q$.
elements.sent-2411-045: The next state and output at each discrete time index $t$ are \begin{equation}\label{eq:sys_h2} x(t+1) = A x(t) + B u(t) + E \xi(t) y(t) = Cx(t) + D u(t). \nonumber\end{equation} Given a static state-feedback controller $u=Kx$, consider the closed-loop system \begin{equation}x(t+1) = (A+BK)x(t)+E\xi(t) y(t) = (C+DK)x(t),\end{equation} and denote the resulting closed-loop transfer function from $\xi$ to $y$ by $G_K(z)$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sys_h2} x(t+1) = A x(t) + B u(t) + E \xi(t) y(t) = Cx(t) + D u(t). \nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: The classical suboptimal $H_2$ control design problem (see e.g. \cite[Chapter 4] {zhou1998essentials ) reads as follows: Given a performance metric $\gamma\geq 0$, design (if it exists) $K \in \R^{n \times m}$ such that $\norm{G_K(z) _{H_2}\leq\gamma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this case, we say $K$ is a $\gamma$-suboptimal $H_2$ controller for \eqref{eq:sys_h2 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The $H_2$ synthesis procedure can be readily cast as an \ac{LMI feasibility problem (see e.g. \cite{boyd1994linear ).}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Here, we follow the approach of \cite{de2002extended , which introduces an additional variable $R$, which will prove useful in the remainder of the paper.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, we can define a new variable $L = K R$, which leads to a linear problem.
elements.sent-2411-051: For this, note that the previous matrix inequality is linear in all its decision variables and in $\gamma^2$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Hence, finding $K$ such that $\gamma$ is minimal takes the form of a semidefinite program (SDP) and can be solved with standard methods.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Convex Sufficient Conditions for Structured Control
elements.sent-2411-053: For many applications, full state feedback controllers are not desirable or are even impossible to implement.
elements.sent-2411-054: As an example, networked systems or networked state-feedback may have limited communication ability, which in turn imposes a sparsity pattern on the set of feasible controller matrices $K$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Here, we consider the problem of structured control design.
elements.sent-2411-056: That is, we restrict ourselves to feedback gains $K\in\mathcal{S}$, where $\mathcal{S \subseteq \mathbb{R ^{m\times n}$ is a given subspace.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: This can be captured in a prescribed elementwise zero-nonzero structure on $K$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The enforced zeros in certain elements of the $K$ matrix could correspond to lack of information or actuation capacity between output and input channels, such as in the networked or multi-agent setting.
elements.sent-2411-059: An example of a sparsity constraint $\s$ is: \begin{equation}\label{eq:sparse} \s = \left\lbrace \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_1 \alpha_2 0 0 \alpha_3 \alpha_4 \end{bmatrix} \quad \Bigg| \quad \alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_4\in\R \right\rbrace.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: If the subspace $\s \subseteq \mathbb{R ^{m\times n $ arises from a sparsity pattern, we will identify it with sparsity pattern $\text{sp (\s)\in \{0,1\}^{m\times n}$, where $0$ denotes an enforced zero and $1$ a free element.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: A common approach for subspaces $\s$ defined by a sparsity pattern, is to impose that $L \in \s$ and $R$ is diagonal.
elements.sent-2411-063: In contrast, the work in \cite{ferrante2019design poses a less conservative sufficient condition to ensure $K \in \s$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The approach in \cite{ferrante2019design generalizes to subspaces $\s$ that do not arise from sparsity patterns, such as the setting of coordinated control in a multi-agent system.}
elements.sent-2411-065: To describe the subspace structure, we first require some notation.
elements.sent-2411-066: Given a basis $\{S_\ell\}_{\ell=1 ^k$ for $\s$ (a set of matrices $S_\ell\in\mathbb{R ^{m\times n}$ such that \begin{equation}\s = \left\{\sum_{\ell=1}^k\alpha_\ell S_\ell \Bigg| \alpha_1,...,\alpha_k\inR\right\},\end{equation} we define a representation matrix $S\in\mathbb{R ^{m\times nk}$ for the subspace $\s$ as the horizontal concatenation of these matrices, \begin{equation}\label{eq:s_basis} S := \begin{bmatrix} S_1 S_2 \ldots S_k \end{bmatrix}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The following lemma is the tool that will allow us to impose structure on controllers.
elements.sent-2411-068: Define $\Upsilon(S)$ as the following set: \begin{equation}\Upsilon(S) := \{Q \in \R^{n \times n} \mid \exists \Lambda \in \psd^k : S(I_k \otimes Q) = S(\Lambda \otimes I_n)\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: The set $\Upsilon(S)$ is a convex set in $Q$ given $S$, given that is the projection of a subspace in $(Q, \Lambda)$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Then, the following holds for all matrices $(L, R)$ such that $R$ is invertible: \begin{equation}L \in \s, \ R \in \Upsilon(S) \implies L R^{-1} \in \s.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: IV.B]{ferrante2019design \begin{equation}sp(\s) =  1 1 0 0 1 1 , R =  R_{11} R_{12} 0 0 R_{22} 0 0 R_{32} R_{33} . \label{eq:r_upsilon}\end{equation} The structure for $R$ in \eqref{eq:r_upsilon arises because the representation $\exists \Lambda: \ S(I_k \otimes Q) = S(\Lambda \otimes I_n)$ imposes that $R_{13 , R_{21 , R_{23 , R_{31 = 0$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}sp(\s) =  1 1 0 0 1 1 , R =  R_{11} R_{12} 0 0 R_{22} 0 0 R_{32} R_{33} . \label{eq:r_upsilon}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: If $R \in \Upsilon(S)$ is invertible, then $L \in \s$ implies $L R^{-1 \in \s$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The notation $R \in \Upsilon(S)$ will be used throughout this paper when presenting optimization problems, noting that $\Lambda$ can an optimization variable or eliminated as appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-074: If the following \ac{LMI s in the variables $P, R, L$ are simultaneously feasible \begin{subequations \begin{equation} P - E E^\top A R + B L (A R + B L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succ 0 \label{eq:h2_aa}  Q CR +D L (CR +D L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succ 0 \label{eq:h2_ab} P \succ 0 Tr(Q) \leq \gamma^2 P \in \psd^n, \ Q \in \psd^q, \ R \in \Upsilon(S), \ L \in \s,\end{equation} \end{subequations then $R$ is nonsingular and $K =L R^{-1 \in \s$ a $\gamma$-suboptimal $H_2$ controller for \eqref{eq:sys_h2 .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} P - E E^\top A R + B L (A R + B L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succ 0 \label{eq:h2_aa}  Q CR +D L (CR +D L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succ 0 \label{eq:h2_ab} P \succ 0 Tr(Q) \leq \gamma^2 P \in \psd^n, \ Q \in \psd^q, \ R \in \Upsilon(S), \ L \in \s,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: In the previous problem formulated in \eqref{eq:h2_structure , we assumed full knowledge of the system matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-076: We now consider the case where the system matrices are not known a priori.
elements.sent-2411-077: To be precise, we assume that the matrices $C$, $D$ and $E$ are chosen by the designer as part of the design goal.
elements.sent-2411-078: Hence, we assume that the state and input matrices $A$ and $B$ have to be determined from measurements.
elements.sent-2411-079: In order to do so, we will assume that we have access to input and state measurements, and the state is also driven by unobserved bounded process noise.
elements.sent-2411-080: A sequence of observations for the time window $t = 1, ...,T$ is collected from the true system $(A_\ast, B_\ast)$ with an unknown process noise $w$: \begin{equation}x(t+1) = \textstyle A_\ast x(t) + B_\ast u(t) + w(t). \label{eq:stab_W}\end{equation} Such observations could arise from a single trajectory or from multiple independent experiments.
elements.sent-2411-081: These observations are assembled into the following matrices: \begin{equation}\label{eq:data} {cccccrl} \xn := [ x(0) x(1) \ldots x(T-1)] \in \R^{n \times T} \bu := [ u(0) u(1) \ldots u(T-1)] \in \R^{m\times T} \bw := [ w(0) w(1) \ldots w(T-1)] \in \R^{n \times T} \xp := [ x(1) x(2) \ldots x(T) ] \in \R^{n \times T}. \end{equation} This yields the expression \begin{equation}\label{eq:data-eq} \xp = A_\ast \xn + B_\ast \bu + \bw.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{eq:data} {cccccrl} \xn := [ x(0) x(1) \ldots x(T-1)] \in \R^{n \times T} \bu := [ u(0) u(1) \ldots u(T-1)] \in \R^{m\times T} \bw := [ w(0) w(1) \ldots w(T-1)] \in \R^{n \times T} \xp := [ x(1) x(2) \ldots x(T) ] \in \R^{n \times T}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: We assume that we have access to the matrices $\xp$, $\xn$, and $\bu$.
elements.sent-2411-083: In contrast, the matrix $\bw$ is unknown, but we assume it is bounded as follows: \begin{asmp The matrix $\bw$ collecting the process noise signal $w(\cdot)$ satisfies \begin{equation}\begin{bmatrix} I \bw^\top \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Phi_{11} \Phi_{12} \Phi_{12}^\top \Phi_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I \bw^\top \end{bmatrix}^\top \succeq 0. \label{eq:qmi_noise}\end{equation} for some $\Phi \in \psd^{n+T}$ with $\Phi_{11 \in \psd_+^n$ and $-\Phi_{22 \in \psd_{++ ^T$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: In particular, a recorded trajectory of length $T$ with an per-time process noise bound of $\epsilon$ ($\forall t \in 0..T-1: \ \norm{w(t) _2 \leq \epsilon)$ can be overapproximated by \eqref{eq:qmi_noise using a $\Phi$ matrix of \begin{equation}\Phi =  T \epsilon I_n \0 \0 -I_T . \label{eq:noise_bound_eps}\end{equation} \end{rmk We now aim to form an expression to bound the consistent plant matrices $(A, B)$ under the bound on $\bw$ from Assumption 1.}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Combining the dynamics relation from \eqref{eq:data-eq and the noise bound in \eqref{eq:qmi_noise , we can define a matrix $\Psi \in \psd^{2n+m}$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:psi_matrix} \Psi :=  I \xp \0 -\xn \0 -\bu ^\top \Phi  I \xp \0 -\xn \0 -\bu .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: By assumption, this set is nonempty, as the true plant $(A_\ast, B_\ast)$ is a member of $\Sigma_\dc$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Recall our objective of designing a structured $\gamma$-suboptimal $H_2$ controller for \eqref{eq:sys_h2 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: We now reason as follows: Given that we can not distinguish the systems in $\Sigma_\dc$ on the basis of the data $\{\xp,\xn,\bu\}$, we can guarantee our objective only if $K\in\s$ and $K$ is a $\gamma$-suboptimal $H_2$ controller for all $(A,B)$ compatible with the data.
elements.sent-2411-089: With these elements in place, we therefore aim at resolving whether there exist $P, R, L, \Lambda$ such that \eqref{eq:h2_structure is feasible for all $(A,B) \in\Sigma_\dc$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Note that, in \eqref{eq:h2_structure , only \eqref{eq:h2_aa depends on $(A,B)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: In order to efficiently test whether \eqref{eq:h2_aa holds for all $(A,B)\in\Sigma_\dc$, we first reformulate the former as a QMI of the same form as \eqref{eq:data_consistency .}}

elements.sent-2411-093: Rearranging the terms yields \begin{equation}P - E E^\top -  A B ^\top  R L (R+R^\top - P)^{-1} R L ^\top A B \succ 0,\end{equation} which can in turn be formulated as \begin{equation} I A^\top B^\top ^{\!\!\top}\!\! P- E E^\top\!\! \0 \0 \!\!\!\!- R L (R+R^\top- P)^{-1} R L ^\top   I A^\top B^\top \succ 0 . \label{eq:sigma_d}\end{equation} Though this inequality is clearly no longer linear in $R$, $P$, $L$, and $E$, it has the same quadratic structure in $A$ and $B$ as \eqref{eq:data_consistency .}

E E^\top \!-\!\beta I\!\! \0 \0 \0 \cr \0 \0 \0 R \cr \0 \0 \0 L \cr\- \0 R^\top L^\top R+R^\top \!-\! P \cr \end{pmat} } \!\!-\! \alpha \begin{pmat}[{|}] \Psi \0 \cr\- \0 \0 \cr \end{pmat}\! \succeq \! 0 \label{eq:pd_data}  Q CR +D L (CR +D L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succeq 0 \label{eq:pd_q} Tr(Q) \leq \gamma^2 \alpha \geq 0, \beta > 0, \gamma \geq 0 P \in \psd^n, \ Q \in \psd^q, \ R \in \Upsilon(S), \ L \in \s.\end{equation}.}

P \cr \end{pmat} } \!\!-\! \alpha \begin{pmat}[{|}] \Psi \0 \cr\- \0 \0 \cr \end{pmat}\! \succeq \! 0 \label{eq:pd_data}  Q CR +D L (CR +D L)^\top R + R^\top - P  \succeq 0 \label{eq:pd_q} Tr(Q) \leq \gamma^2 \alpha \geq 0, \beta > 0, \gamma \geq 0 P \in \psd^n, \ Q \in \psd^q, \ R \in \Upsilon(S), \ L \in \s.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-097: In turn, we can conclude by Lemma~\ref{lem:struc h2 , that $K = LR^{-1}$ is a $\gamma^*$-suboptimal $H_2$ controller for all systems $(A,B)\in \Sigma_\dc$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Hence, this theorem provides a method to guarantee performance of a controller for the underlying measured system.
elements.sent-2411-099: Since, in addition the only non-conservative step leading up to Theorem~\ref{thm:main is the convex relaxation based on Lemma~\ref{lem:structure , this in turn is also the only source of conservatism in this data-based controller design procedure.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: In order to implement \eqref{eq:h2_structure_dd on computational devices, the positive definiteness constraints in \eqref{eq:pd_data and \eqref{eq:pd_q must be replaced by positive semidefinite constraints with respect to a positive tolerance $\eta$ (as in $\succeq \eta I$).}}}

elements.sent-2411-102: The matrix in \eqref{eq:pd_data has size $3n+m$, and the matrix in \eqref{eq:pd_q has size $q+n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: MATLAB (2024a) code to generate all examples is publicly available at \url{https://www.doi.org/10.3929/ethz-b-000702171 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Dependencies for these routines include Mosek \cite{mosek92 and YALMIP \cite{lofberg2004yalmip .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: All experiments will employ bounded noise described by the $\Phi$ from \eqref{eq:noise_bound_eps with appropriate choices of $T$ and $\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: A numerical tolerance of $\eta = 10^{-3}$ is used to enforce positive definiteness of requisite strict LMI constraints.
elements.sent-2411-107: This example involves $H_2$-suboptimal control of the following ground-truth plant with $n=3$ states and $m=2$ inputs: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}A_\ast =  -0.4095 0.4036 -0.0874 0.5154 -0.0815 0.1069 1.6715 0.7718 -0.3376  \nonumber B_\ast =  0 0 -0.6359 -0.1098 -0.0325 2.2795 . \label{eq:exmp_1_sys} \intertext{We aim at designing a controller $K$ with the sparsity pattern} sp(\s) =  1 1 0 0 1 1 ,\end{equation} in which the corresponding $R$ matrix has a structure observed in \eqref{eq:r_upsilon from Example \ref{exmp:sparse .}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation}A_\ast =  -0.4095 0.4036 -0.0874 0.5154 -0.0815 0.1069 1.6715 0.7718 -0.3376  \nonumber B_\ast =  0 0 -0.6359 -0.1098 -0.0325 2.2795 . \label{eq:exmp_1_sys} \intertext{We aim at designing a controller $K$ with the sparsity pattern} sp(\s) =  1 1 0 0 1 1 ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-108: The $H_2$-suboptimal control problem is specified by the following matrices: \begin{equation}C =  I_3 \0_{2 \times 3} , D =  \0_{3 \times 2} I_2 , E = I_3.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-109: The numerical experiments consider the following design choices for convexly synthesized structured control via LMI \eqref{eq:h2_structure_dd :.}
elements.sent-2411-110: A dense matrix $P$ with $P=R$ and $L \in \R^{m \times n}$;.
elements.sent-2411-114: Design 1 performs $H_2$ suboptimal control without applying a structural constraint on the gain matrix $K$.
elements.sent-2411-115: The focus of this work is on Design 4.
elements.sent-2411-116: Design 1 is included to compare against the structure-free control cost.
elements.sent-2411-117: Designs 2 and 3 add more rigid constraints on the $H_2$ program when applying structured control as compared to our proposed Design 4.
elements.sent-2411-118: Designs 2 and 3 are used as a reference method to compare our contribution in Design 4, since the problem of data-driven structured control has not previously been considered in prior work.
elements.sent-2411-119: Table \ref{tab:exmp_1_eps reports suboptimal $H_2$ bounds for this system with increasing $\epsilon$ under fixed $T=20$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Table \ref{tab:exmp_1 similarly reports $H_2$ bounds with increasing $T$ under fixed $\epsilon=0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-121: All sampled trajectories begin at the initial point $x(0) = \begin{bmatrix 1 0 0\end{bmatrix ^\top$.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The column heading $(A_\ast, B_\ast)$ refers to solving \ac{LMI \eqref{eq:h2_design with respect to the ground truth system under the restrictions described by the design.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: In Table \ref{tab:exmp_1_eps , different trajectories of length $T=20$ are sampled under the different values of $\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Increasing $\epsilon$ leads to a greater certified closed-loop $H_2$ bound for each implementation.
elements.sent-2411-125: The structured control of Design 4 ($R \in \Upsilon(S)$) reduces this $H_2$ bounds as compared to the existing approaches for structured control in Designs 2 and 3.
elements.sent-2411-126: In Table \ref{tab:exmp_1 , a single underlying trajectory of length $T=20$ is used with noise $\epsilon=0.1$, in which the data set $\mathcal{D}$ is formed by keeping to the first $k$ samples in the given trajectory (heading $T = k$).}
elements.sent-2411-127: In the case where $T=5$, the data-driven \ac{LMI in \eqref{eq:h2_structure_dd is infeasible for all Designs.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: It is worth noting that increasing the data length $T$ can lead to more conservative solutions: this is due to the approximation of the individual sample noise bound by a single ellipsoid \cite{bisoffi2024controller .}
elements.sent-2411-129: This lack of monotonic decrease of $H_2$ the bound is observed in Table \ref{tab:exmp_1 , in which the $T=20$ bounds are higher than the $T=6$ bounds for Designs 2-4.}
elements.sent-2411-130: Even so, Design 4 maintains a lower $H_2$ bound than Designs 2-3.
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Closed-loop $H_2$ bounds for the system in \eqref{eq:exmp_1} v.s. $\epsilon$ with $T=20$} \begin{tabular}{l|c c c c} Design $(A_\ast, B_\ast)$ $\epsilon=0.05$ $\epsilon=0.1$ $\epsilon=0.15$ 1 (Unstructured) 2.1537 2.3448 3.0939 4.5757 2 ($P=R$ diag.) 3.5658 4.6619 7.4193 Infeasible 3 ($R$ diag.) 3.0089 3.5997 4.9506 9.1999 4 ($R \in \Upsilon(S)$) 2.9794 3.5495 4.6806 8.9710 \end{tabular} \label{tab:exmp_1_eps}
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Closed-loop $H_2$ bounds for the system in \eqref{eq:exmp_1} v.s. $T$ with $\epsilon = 0.1$} \begin{tabular}{l|c c c c} Design $(A_\ast, B_\ast)$ $T=6$ $T=10$ $T=20$ 1 (Unstructured) 2.1537 2.9911 2.8156 3.0939 2 ($P=R$ diag.) 3.5658 6.3386 7.0963 7.4193 3 ($R$ diag.) 3.0089 4.5545 4.5044 4.9506 4 ($R \in \Upsilon(S)$) 2.9794 4.4036 4.4323 4.6806 \end{tabular} \label{tab:exmp_1}
elements.sent-2411-131: The case of (Design 4, $T=6$) leads to the following example solution ($Q$ omitted for space): \begin{subequations \begin{equation}P =  2.4636 -0.2394 -2.2023 -0.2394 1.6204 0.5241 -2.2023 0.5241 11.0167  R =  2.7105 -0.0045 0 0 1.6720 0 0 1.4480 12.9819  L =  1.4524 0.3110 0 0 -0.7219 2.8893  K =  0.5359 0.1875 0 0 -0.6245 0.2226  \alpha = 8.8405, \qquad \beta = 1.0018\times 10^{-5}.\end{equation}. \end{subequations.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: The second example involves a system with $n=12$ states and $m=6$ inputs.
elements.sent-2411-133: The sparsity pattern in this example is \begin{subequations \begin{equation}sp(\s) = \1_{3 \times 2} \1_{3 \times 8} \0_{3 \times 2} \0_{3 \times 2} \1_{3 \times 8} \1_{3 \times 2} ,\end{equation} and we aim at finding a sharing control policy ($\sum_{j=1 ^m u_j(t) = 0$ as enforced by $\1^\top K = \0$) following the sparsity structure in \eqref{eq:exmp_2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: This sharing control can be enforced by adding the constraint $\1^\top L = \0$ to the $H_2$ LMI programs \eqref{eq:h2_design and \eqref{eq:h2_structure_dd .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Similar to before, we aim at minimizing the gain between the noise and the signal $\begin{bmatrix x^\top u^\top\end{bmatrix ^\top$, that is, we take \begin{equation}C =  I_{12} \0_{6 \times 12} , D =  \0_{12 \times 6} I_6 , E = I_{12}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Table \ref{tab:exmp_2_eps lists structured $H_2$ bounds under the Designs when $T=100$ and $\epsilon$ increases.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Table \ref{tab:exmp_2 similarly reports structured $H_2$ bounds for varying $T$ under a noise bound of $\epsilon=0.05$.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Tables \ref{tab:exmp_2_eps and \ref{tab:exmp_2 echo the results of Tables \ref{tab:exmp_1_eps and \ref{tab:exmp_1 respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: For this larger example, Designs 2 and 3 are always infeasible (over the course of the presented experiments), while Design 4 can return feasible $H_2$ values (outside of $T=100, \epsilon = 0.08$).
elements.sent-2411-140: The lack of monotonic decrease of the $H_2$ norm is again observed for Design 4 in Table \ref{tab:exmp_2 .}
